─[1]-Project──
lorehq
─[2]-Primitives──
Rules Skills Agents Fieldnotes Runbooks
─[3]-Guides──
CommandsCustomizationGuidesPlatform SupportWorking Across Repos
─[4]-Workflow──
Active Work Architecture
─[ Docs Site Restructure ]─ ┐

Docs Site Restructure

Break the lore-docs public site into atomic fragments of truth, classify each by Diátaxis type, reassemble into a rule-compliant navigation structure, and verify nothing useful was lost. Uses a semantic search container as a content integrity oracle throughout.

WARNING: This runbook restructures the entire public documentation site. Run on a clean git state with a dedicated branch. Review each phase output before approving execution.

Prerequisites

  • lore-docs checked out locally (/home/andrew/Github/lore-docs)
  • Source repos available: lore/, create-lore/, lore-docker/
  • Docker available for the integrity oracle container
  • Rule loaded: docs/context/rules/documentation-site-structure.md
  • Docs rule: docs/context/rules/documentation.md
  • Clean git state in lore-docs: git status must show no uncommitted changes
  • Create a branch: git checkout -b docs-restructure-$(date +%Y%m%d)

What Is “Useful and Relevant”

Content is useful and relevant if it meets any of these criteria:

  1. Actionable — a reader could follow it to accomplish something (install, configure, debug, migrate, deploy)
  2. Factual and verifiable — states a specific claim about system behavior, configuration, or architecture that can be verified against source code
  3. Decision-supporting — helps a reader choose between alternatives (when to use, comparison tables, trade-offs, limitations)
  4. Empirical — contains original measurements, test results, or data that cannot be derived from source code alone
  5. Unique — the information exists only on this page (not restated from another page or derivable from source)

Content is not useful if it is:

  • Restated — says the same thing as another page (delete the duplicate, keep the canonical version, link to it)
  • Vague filler — “Lore is great for teams” without specifics, or marketing-style claims without evidence
  • Stale — contradicts current source code and the correct version exists elsewhere
  • Orphaned internal detail — implementation specifics that only matter if reading source (e.g., variable names, internal function signatures) and are not needed for user-facing tasks
  • Scaffolding — empty index pages, placeholder sections, “coming soon” stubs with no content

The integrity oracle (Phase 0) indexes all content before restructuring. After restructuring, every fragment that met the “useful” criteria must be locatable via semantic search against the new site. Fragments that were deleted as not-useful are logged with the reason for deletion so the operator can review.

Protected Pages

Some pages contain empirical research data. These are primary sources — not derived from code, not candidates for reduction or restructuring.

Protected page criteria:

  • Contains original test data, measurements, or statistical results
  • Presents methodology + findings from controlled experiments
  • Explicitly cites sample sizes, conditions, and limitations

Current protected pages (entire cost-evidence/ section):

  • cost-evidence/index.md
  • cost-evidence/analysis.md
  • cost-evidence/methodology.md
  • cost-evidence/raw-data.md
  • cost-evidence/operator-inputs.md

Workers must not fragment, merge, reduce, or relocate protected pages. They move as a unit into whatever nav section the structure proposal assigns them.

Target Navigation Structure

The restructured site must use Diátaxis-aligned tabs with a shallow sidebar. The rule (documentation-site-structure.md) defines the rules; this section defines the concrete target for lore-docs.

Tab: Getting Started        (Diátaxis: Tutorial)
  - Installation + first "aha moment" (under 5 minutes)
  - First Session Setup (profiles)
  - Migration paths

Tab: Guides                 (Diátaxis: How-to)
  - Working with Lore
  - Working Across Repos
  - Configuration
  - Docs UI & Search
  - Rules
  - Roadmaps, Plans, Notes & Brainstorms
  - Platform-specific guides (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenCode)
  - Troubleshooting

Tab: Concepts               (Diátaxis: Explanation)
  - How It Works (harness engineering, architecture)
  - How Delegation Works (orchestrator/worker, session acceleration)
  - Hook Architecture (lifecycle, module layout)
  - Security Model (layers, supply chain)
  - When to Use Lore (fit/poor fit, comparisons)
  - Production Readiness (stability, limitations, trust model)

Tab: Reference              (Diátaxis: Reference)
  - Commands (slash-command reference)
  - Configuration Reference (all config fields, env vars)
  - Hook Reference (per-hook spec: event, purpose, inputs, outputs)
  - Platform Feature Matrix
  - Examples (skill, roadmap, plan, note, brainstorm templates)

Tab: Evidence               (Protected — moves as a unit)
  - Cost Evidence (overview, analysis, methodology, raw data, operator inputs)

  Changelog (top-level, no tab — or under Reference)

This is a starting proposal. Phase 3 produces the actual structure based on fragment analysis. The operator approves before execution.

Phase 0: Integrity Oracle Setup (sequential)

Start a semantic search container indexing the current site content. This becomes the “before” snapshot for integrity verification.

# Index the current lore-docs site
docker run -d --name lore-restructure-before \
  -p 9192:8080 \
  -e MAX_K=15 \
  -v /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs/docs:/data/docs \
  lorehq/lore-docker:latest

# Wait for healthy + indexed
until curl -sf http://localhost:9192/health | grep -q '"ok":true'; do sleep 5; done
echo "Before-snapshot indexed"

MAX_K=15 — fragments may be small; higher K ensures nothing is missed in verification queries.

Do not proceed until the container reports healthy and indexing is complete.

Phase 1: Fragment (parallel — haiku x4)

Break every page into atomic fragments of truth. Each fragment is one self-contained fact, instruction, or explanation.

Split all non-protected doc pages into ~4 groups by topic area. Each worker loads:

  • docs/context/rules/documentation-site-structure.md only

What a fragment is

A fragment is the smallest unit of content that is independently meaningful:

  • A single factual claim (“Lore has zero npm runtime dependencies”)
  • A single instruction (“Run /lore-docker to start the sidecar”)
  • A single configuration entry (field name, type, default, description)
  • A single comparison row (Lore vs alternative on one dimension)
  • A single troubleshooting entry (symptom + fix)
  • A single code example with its immediate context sentence
  • A diagram + its caption (diagrams are never split from their labels)

Fragments that require each other to make sense stay together (e.g., an “if X then Y” pair, or a code block with its prerequisite).

Worker instructions

For each page in their group:

  1. Read the full page
  2. Assess against the “useful and relevant” criteria (see above)
  3. Split into fragments, preserving:
    • The exact source text (verbatim — no rewriting in this phase)
    • Source file and line range
    • Fragment type: fact, instruction, config-entry, comparison, troubleshoot, code-example, diagram, explanation, heading-only
  4. Classify each fragment’s Diátaxis type: tutorial, how-to, reference, explanation
  5. Flag fragments that are not-useful with a reason (restated, vague, stale, orphaned, scaffolding)

Worker return format

[
  {
    "source_file": "how-it-works.md",
    "source_lines": "9-14",
    "text": "Prompt engineering is about crafting individual prompts...",
    "type": "explanation",
    "diataxis": "explanation",
    "useful": true,
    "topics": ["harness-engineering", "architecture"],
    "depends_on": []
  },
  {
    "source_file": "getting-started.md",
    "source_lines": "15-18",
    "text": "```bash\nnpx create-lore my-project\ncd my-project\n```",
    "type": "instruction",
    "diataxis": "tutorial",
    "useful": true,
    "topics": ["installation"],
    "depends_on": []
  }
]

Orchestrator merges all worker outputs into a single fragment inventory.

Fragment counts

Estimate ~200-400 fragments across the full site (~25 content pages). If any worker returns more than 150 fragments for its group, the pages were likely over-fragmented — flag for orchestrator review.

Phase 2: Classify and Deduplicate (single sonnet)

One sonnet worker receives the full fragment inventory. Worker loads:

  • docs/context/rules/documentation-site-structure.md only

Tasks

  1. Deduplicate — identify fragments that state the same fact. For each duplicate set, mark one as canonical and the rest as duplicate-of: <canonical_id>. The canonical version is the one with the most specific, complete, and accurate phrasing.

  2. Validate Diátaxis classification — review fragment classifications. Common misclassifications:

    • “How it works” explanation mixed into a how-to guide
    • Reference-style config tables embedded in tutorials
    • Tutorial hand-holding inside reference sections

  3. Dependency mapping — identify fragments that must appear on the same page (e.g., a config key and its “see also” link, a code example and its prerequisite step).

  4. Gap detection — compare the fragment set against the target navigation structure. Identify sections that have no fragments assigned (gaps in current content) and fragments that don’t fit any target section (content without a home).

Worker return format

Updated fragment inventory with:

  • canonical or duplicate-of markers
  • Corrected diataxis classifications where changed
  • page_group — proposed target page in the new structure
  • gap_report — sections with no content, fragments without a home

Phase 3: Structure Proposal (single sonnet)

One sonnet worker receives the classified fragment inventory and gap report. Worker loads:

  • docs/context/rules/documentation-site-structure.md
  • docs/context/rules/documentation.md

Tasks

  1. Propose the full nav structure — concrete mkdocs.yml nav tree with tabs, sections, and pages. Every page must have a clear Diátaxis type.

  2. Assign fragments to pages — each canonical (non-duplicate, non-not-useful) fragment maps to exactly one page in the new structure.

  3. Propose page outlines — for each page, list the fragments in reading order with section headings. Target 800–3,000 words per page; flag pages outside that range.

  4. Produce the move map — for every current file:

    • Which new file(s) its fragments land in
    • Which fragments were dropped (with reason)
    • Which new files are created (with fragment sources)

  5. Produce the deletion log — every fragment marked not-useful or duplicate-of with its full text and deletion reason. This is the operator’s audit trail for “was anything lost.”

  6. Produce the mkdocs.yml theme update — add navigation.tabs, navigation.tabs.sticky, navigation.indexes, navigation.path, navigation.instant, navigation.prune. Remove navigation.sections if using tabs (sections apply within tabs automatically).

Worker return format

  • Proposed nav: section for mkdocs.yml
  • Proposed theme.features: section for mkdocs.yml
  • Page outline per new page (heading + fragment IDs)
  • Full move map (old file → new file(s))
  • Full deletion log (dropped fragments + reasons)

Phase 4: Operator Review (sequential — orchestrator)

Present to operator:

  1. Before/after nav tree — side by side
  2. Page count change — current pages vs. proposed pages
  3. Deletion log summary — count of dropped fragments, grouped by reason (duplicate, vague, stale, orphaned, scaffolding)
  4. Full deletion log — every dropped fragment with text and reason (operator scans for false positives)
  5. Gap report — any target sections with no content (these become stubs or are deferred)
  6. Pages outside 800–3,000 word range — with justification
  7. Mixed-Diátaxis pages — any page that contains fragments from multiple Diátaxis types (should be zero; if not, explain why)

Do not proceed until operator explicitly approves. Operator may:

  • Rescue fragments from the deletion log (mark as useful, assign to a page)
  • Reject specific page merges or splits
  • Adjust the nav structure
  • Add/remove tabs

Phase 5: Assemble (parallel — sonnet x3-4)

Write the new pages. Split into groups by tab (Getting Started, Guides, Concepts, Reference). Each worker loads:

  • docs/context/rules/documentation-site-structure.md
  • docs/context/rules/documentation.md
  • Its assigned page outlines with fragment texts

Rules

  • Fragments are the raw material, not the final text. Workers assemble fragments into coherent pages with proper flow, transitions, and section structure. They may rephrase for clarity but must not add new facts, remove facts, or change meaning.
  • One Diátaxis type per page. If a fragment doesn’t fit the page’s type, it should have been caught in Phase 2. Flag and skip.
  • Cross-link aggressively. Every mention of a concept, command, or page that exists elsewhere gets a relative link.
  • End each page with “Next steps” or “See also” — related pages the reader might want next.
  • Section index pages — every nav section (tab root, subsection) gets an index.md that orients and links to children. Keep under 30 lines.
  • Protected pages — copy as-is to their new location. Do not modify content. Update only internal links if paths changed.

Worker return format

Complete page content (markdown) for each assigned page, ready to write to disk.

Phase 6: Write and Relink (sequential — orchestrator)

  1. Write new pages — write every page to the new file structure under docs/
  2. Write mkdocs.yml — replace the nav: and theme.features: sections
  3. Remove old files — git rm every file that was fully consumed by the new structure and has no remaining content
  4. Link repair — scan all new pages for relative links. Build a { old_path: new_path } map from the move map. Replace every old-path link with the corresponding new-path link.
  5. Redirect stubs — for pages that had external links pointing to them (README cross-references, GitHub wiki links, the lore source repo), create a stub at the old path that redirects or links to the new location. These prevent broken external links.

Phase 7: Integrity Verification (sequential)

7a — Build check

cd /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs
mkdocs build --strict   # Catches broken links, missing nav entries, YAML errors

Fix any build errors before proceeding.

7b — Semantic integrity check

Start a second search container indexing the new site content.

docker run -d --name lore-restructure-after \
  -p 9193:8080 \
  -e MAX_K=15 \
  -v /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs/docs:/data/docs \
  lorehq/lore-docker:latest

until curl -sf http://localhost:9193/health | grep -q '"ok":true'; do sleep 5; done
echo "After-snapshot indexed"

7c — Coverage sweep (parallel — sonnet x2)

For every canonical fragment from Phase 1 that was marked useful:

  1. Query the before container (port 9192) with the fragment’s key terms → confirm it was findable in the old site (baseline sanity check)
  2. Query the after container (port 9193) with the same terms → confirm it is findable in the new site
  3. For hits on the after container: read the matched page and verify the fragment’s meaning is preserved (not just keyword-matched but semantically equivalent)

Workers load:

  • No rule — this is a verification pass, not a writing pass

Worker return format

| Fragment ID | Before Hit? | After Hit? | Meaning Preserved? | Notes |

Any fragment that is useful=true and either After Hit = No or Meaning Preserved = No is a regression. Report to orchestrator.

7d — Regression triage (sequential — orchestrator)

If regressions exist:

  1. Read the fragment and the target page
  2. Determine if the content was accidentally dropped, mangled, or just indexed differently
  3. Fix: re-insert the fragment into the appropriate page
  4. Re-run mkdocs build --strict after fixes

Present regression count and fixes to operator.

Phase 8: Polish (parallel — sonnet x2-3)

Rule compliance sweep on the assembled pages. Each worker loads:

  • docs/context/rules/documentation.md only

Each worker scans for:

  • Filler phrases (“it should be noted”, “in order to”, “basically”)
  • Vague pronouns (“it”, “this”, “that” when referent is ambiguous)
  • Pages over 150 lines (rule limit)
  • Mixed topics on one page
  • Terminology inconsistencies
  • Stale TODOs or placeholders
  • Missing cross-links (mentions of concepts without links)

Worker return format: punch list with exact current text and replacement text.

Orchestrator executes edits, then re-runs mkdocs build --strict.

Phase 9: Teardown (sequential)

docker stop lore-restructure-before lore-restructure-after
docker rm lore-restructure-before lore-restructure-after

Phase 10: Commit (sequential)

cd /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs
git add docs/ mkdocs.yml
git diff --cached --stat   # Review magnitude
git commit -m "Restructure docs site: Diátaxis-aligned tabs, fragment-and-reassemble"

Do not push until operator reviews the final diff.

Model Allocation

PhaseModelWorkersRuleRationale
Phase 1 FragmentHaiku4site-structureStructured extraction; fast and cheap
Phase 2 ClassifySonnet1site-structureDeduplication and classification require judgment
Phase 3 StructureSonnet1site-structure + docsArchitectural decision on nav and page grouping
Phase 5 AssembleSonnet3–4site-structure + docsWriting coherent pages from fragments
Phase 7c VerifySonnet2noneSemantic comparison requires comprehension
Phase 8 PolishSonnet2–3docsRule compliance is a judgment call

Parallelization Diagram

Phase 0 (setup):       [before-search :9192]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 1 (fragment):    [haiku-1] [haiku-2] [haiku-3] [haiku-4]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 2 (classify):    [sonnet — single worker]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 3 (structure):   [sonnet — single worker]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 4 (review):      [operator approval — blocking gate]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 5 (assemble):    [sonnet-1] [sonnet-2] [sonnet-3] [sonnet-4]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 6 (write):       [orchestrator — sequential]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 7 (verify):      [build] → [after-search :9193] → [sonnet-1] [sonnet-2]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 8 (polish):      [sonnet-1] [sonnet-2] [sonnet-3]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 9 (teardown):    [stop + rm containers]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Phase 10 (commit):     [operator reviews diff → commit]
                       ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Validation:            [structure] [diátaxis] [links] [completeness] [sync] → [sign-off]

Integrity Guarantees

RiskMitigation
Content dropped silentlyDeletion log tracks every removed fragment with reason; operator reviews before execution
Meaning changed during assemblyPhase 7c compares fragment meaning against new pages via semantic search
Broken internal linksmkdocs build --strict catches broken links; Phase 6 runs systematic link repair
Broken external linksPhase 6 creates redirect stubs at old paths
Protected data modifiedProtected pages move as a unit; workers skip them in fragment/classify phases
Duplicate content survivesPhase 2 explicitly deduplicates; canonical version chosen by specificity
Nav structure violates rulePhase 3 worker loads the site-structure rule; operator reviews proposed nav

Validation Review

After commit, before push — a final end-to-end audit of the restructured site.

Structure validation

cd /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs

# Build must pass clean
mkdocs build --strict

# No page over 150 lines (rule limit)
find docs -name "*.md" -exec sh -c 'lines=$(wc -l < "$1"); [ "$lines" -gt 150 ] && echo "OVER: $1 ($lines lines)"' _ {} \;

# Every nav section has an index.md
grep -oP '(?<=- )[\w/]+(?=/index\.md)' mkdocs.yml | while read dir; do
  [ ! -f "docs/$dir/index.md" ] && echo "MISSING INDEX: docs/$dir/index.md"
done

# No orphaned pages (in docs/ but not in nav)
find docs -name "*.md" -not -path "*/stylesheets/*" -not -path "*/assets/*" | sort > /tmp/all-pages.txt
grep -oP '[^\s:]+\.md' mkdocs.yml | sort > /tmp/nav-pages.txt
comm -23 /tmp/all-pages.txt /tmp/nav-pages.txt
# Any output = orphaned page not in nav

Diátaxis compliance

For each tab, verify no page violates its Diátaxis type:

TabAllowed contentViolation signals
Getting StartedSteps the reader follows sequentiallyConfig tables, architectural explanation, exhaustive option lists
GuidesTask-oriented “how to do X”Background theory, spec-style parameter tables, learning-oriented hand-holding
ConceptsBackground, architecture, “why”Step-by-step instructions, parameter defaults, troubleshooting steps
ReferenceExhaustive specificationNarrative prose, opinion, conceptual explanation

Spot-check 2–3 pages per tab. If a page contains more than one paragraph of the wrong type, it needs splitting — create a follow-up item.

Cross-reference integrity

cd /home/andrew/Github/lore-docs

# Find all relative markdown links and verify targets exist
grep -rn '\]([^http][^)]*\.md' docs/ | while IFS=: read file line content; do
  link=$(echo "$content" | grep -oP '\]\(\K[^)]+\.md[^)]*' | head -1)
  # Strip anchor
  target=$(echo "$link" | sed 's/#.*//')
  resolved=$(cd "$(dirname "$file")" && realpath --quiet "$target" 2>/dev/null || echo "MISSING")
  [ "$resolved" = "MISSING" ] && echo "BROKEN: $file:$line → $link"
done

External link preservation

Verify old paths that had inbound links still resolve:

# Key paths referenced from lore source README, lore-docs README, and GitHub links
for path in \
  getting-started.md \
  how-it-works.md \
  how-delegation-works.md \
  security.md \
  when-to-use-lore.md \
  production-readiness.md \
  troubleshooting.md \
  cost-evidence/index.md \
  guides/configuration.md \
  guides/platforms/index.md \
  guides/hook-architecture.md \
  guides/interaction.md \
  guides/cross-repo-workflow.md \
  changelog.md; do
  [ ! -f "docs/$path" ] && echo "MISSING REDIRECT: docs/$path"
done

Any missing path needs a redirect stub (a page with a link to the new location) or the external reference needs updating. Check the lore source repo README and lore-docs README for stale links.

Content completeness

Compare fragment counts against the final site:

  1. Total useful fragments from Phase 1 — this is the baseline
  2. Fragments with confirmed “after” hits from Phase 7c — should be 100%
  3. Regressions fixed in Phase 7d — should be 0 remaining after fixes
  4. Deletion log entries — review count by reason category:
ReasonExpected rangeAction if outside range
Duplicate10–30% of total fragmentsIf over 30%, check that canonical versions are actually comprehensive
Vague filler5–15%If over 15%, verify useful context wasn’t misclassified
StaleUnder 5%If over 5%, run docs-code-alignment-sweep to verify remaining content
Orphaned internalUnder 5%Should be near zero for a user-facing docs site
ScaffoldingUnder 5%Expected — empty stubs and “coming soon” markers

If any category is significantly outside its expected range, review a sample of 5 entries from that category before approving the push.

Bidirectional sync check

The restructure changes file paths. Update the sync infrastructure:

  1. bidirectional-code-docs-sync.md — verify the “Overlap Points” table still has correct lore-docs paths
  2. docs-code-alignment-sweep.md — verify Phase 1 and Phase 3b doc search assumptions still hold with the new file layout
  3. lore source repo README — update any links to lore-docs pages that moved
  4. lore-docs README — update if it references specific doc paths

Create follow-up items for any sync updates needed.

Sign-off

Present to operator:

  • mkdocs build --strict passes
  • No pages over 150 lines
  • Every nav section has an index.md
  • No orphaned pages outside nav
  • Spot-check confirms Diátaxis compliance (no mixed-type pages)
  • No broken internal links
  • All key external paths preserved or redirected
  • Fragment coverage is 100% (all useful fragments accounted for)
  • Deletion log reviewed — no false positives
  • Sync infrastructure updated or follow-ups created

Operator approves push after all checks pass.

Cadence

This is a one-time restructure, not a recurring sweep. After execution:

  • Use docs-code-alignment-sweep.md for ongoing accuracy maintenance
  • Use bidirectional-code-docs-sync.md for keeping docs in sync with code changes
  • The site-structure rule (documentation-site-structure.md) governs all future page additions
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
Donate 0.24.4
